verbatim: Such were their sufferings, when they were conquered by the leader of the Syracusans—a fate which flung their young men from their swift ships into the sea, delivering Hellas from grievous bondage. [§1]
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the military victory of Hieron as a means of delivering the Greek people (Hellas) from bondage/slavery under foreign powers like the Carthaginians and Etruscans.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to live under ancestral laws
verbatim: The descendants of Pamphylus, and, truly, of the Heracleidae also, dwelling beneath the cliffs of Taÿgetus, are willing to abide forever as Dorians under the ordinances of Aegimius. [§1]
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author identifies specific lineage groups (descendants of Pamphylus and Heracleidae) who have the right and will to live governed by their specific ancestral Doric ordinances.

verbatim: Phalaris, with his pitiless mind, who burned his victims in a bronze bull, is surrounded on all sides by a hateful reputation; lyres that resound beneath the roof do not welcome him as a theme in gentle partnership with the voices of boys. [§1]
extracted_rules.reasoning: While Phalaris was a tyrant, the context of 'voices of boys' and household lyres refers to the cultural education and celebration of free-born youth; those who violate social norms (like Phalaris) or lack status are excluded from this immaterial resource of honor.

verbatim: A crafty citizen is unable to speak a compelling word among noble men; and yet he fawns on everyone, weaving complete destruction. I do not share his boldness. [§2]
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text suggests that within the social structure, 'crafty' or lower-status citizens lack the standing to exert real influence or 'speak compelling words' when among the noble class.

verbatim: But Rhadamanthys has prospered, because his allotted portion was the blameless fruit of intelligence, and he does not delight his inner spirit with deceptions, the kind that always follow a man because of the schemes of whisperers. [§2]
extracted_rules.reasoning: Using the mythological figure of Rhadamanthys as a model for Hieron, the text establishes that noble leaders who possess intelligence and avoid deception have the right to stable prosperity.

verbatim: [§3] But she made light of Apollo, in the error of her mind, and consented to another marriage without her father’s knowledge, although she had before lain with Phoebus of the unshorn hair, and was bearing within her the pure seed of the god.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text frames Coronis's decision to marry without her father's knowledge as a moral and legal 'error' and 'lawless deceit', implying that a woman's right to enter a marriage is legally or socially contingent upon paternal consent.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to perform in religious choruses
verbatim: [§3] She did not wait for the marriage-feast to come, nor for the full-voiced cry of the hymenaeal chorus, such things as unmarried girls her own age love to murmur in evening songs to their companion.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author identifies a specific ritual role (the hymenaeal chorus and evening songs) that is the standard domain of unmarried girls of a certain age, establishing it as a normative social resource for that group.

verbatim: [§3] We know of Nestor and Lycian Sarpedon, whom men speak of, from melodious words which skilled craftsmen join together. Through renowned songs excellence gains a long life.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a rule of Greek aristocratic society where rulers and heroes (like Nestor or the 'tyrant' Hieron addressed) gain the resource of 'lofty fame' and 'long life' through the works of poets ('skilled craftsmen').

verbatim: [§3] I would have persuaded him to send even now a healer to cure noble men of their feverish diseases, someone called a son of Apollo or of his father Zeus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The poet specifies that the intended beneficiary of the divine healer's skill would be 'noble men,' suggesting that high-level medical expertise was a resource specifically directed toward the elite.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§4] There they displayed their prowess of limbs in athletic contests with a cloak for a prize, and they went to bed with the women.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes physical competition as a male activity ('displayed their prowess of limbs') where women serve as the rewards or sexual partners after the contest, reflecting the historical exclusion of women from major Greek athletic games.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold the royal scepter and throne
extracted_rules.verbatim: [§4] I have come to my home to recover the ancient honor of my father, now held improperly... But as for the royal scepter and the throne, in which Aeson son of Cretheus once sat, and dispensed straight justice for a nation of horsemen: without any distress between us, release these to me.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Jason claims the throne based on his lineage as the son of the former ruler, asserting that the resource of political power is legally bound to the direct descendants of the previous king.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§4] Cyrene and the most renowned hall of Battus recognized the just mind of Damophilus; a young man among boys, and in counsels like an elder who has lived a hundred years.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The praise for Damophilus stems from his ability to provide counsel equivalent to an elder, implying that the capacity for wise counsel and political influence is a resource typically associated with and reserved for the older age group.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§4] if it comes at last to the winter fire; or if it is placed with upright columns belonging to a ruler, performing a slavish service among foreign walls, having deserted its native place.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Through the metaphor of the oak tree, the text identifies 'slavish service' as being compelled to provide structural/manual support for a ruler's property, identifying the group (slaves) as lacking the resource of bodily autonomy.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§4] Then he would tell you, Arcesilas, what a fountain of immortal song he found, when he was recently entertained by his host at Thebes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The closing lines describe the formal exchange of hospitality and song between the poet and the king/nobility, a resource of social and cultural recognition tied to high status.

verbatim: [§5] He did not bring with him Excuse, the daughter of late-thinking Afterthought, when he came to the house of the descendants of Battus who rule by right; but he was welcomed beside the waters of Castalia, and he flung over your hair the prize of honor for the victorious chariot
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific lineage (the Battiads) as having an inherent right to rule the city of Cyrene.

verbatim: [§6] first of the gods, worship the son of Cronus, the loud-voiced ruler of lightning and thunder; and never deprive your parents of such honor during their allotted lifetime.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a religious and social precept (attributed to Chiron) that children are under a strict obligation to provide honor/support to their parents.

verbatim: [§5] He was blessed when he dwelled among men, and thereafter a hero worshipped by the people. Apart from him, in front of the houses, are the other sacred kings who took their allotted places in Hades, and somehow below the earth they hear, in their minds, great excellence sprinkled with gentle dew by the outpourings of victory-songs
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes the transition of deceased kings into 'heroes' who are worshipped by the community, a distinct immaterial resource (cult status) reserved for this group.

verbatim: [§5] It is fitting for him, in the song of the young men, to celebrate Phoebus with his golden sword, now that he has received from Pytho the graceful victory-song as a compensation for his expense.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Participation in the Pythian games as a chariot owner required massive personal expenditure ('expense'), functioning as a socio-economic rule where the wealthy trade material resources for public honor.

verbatim: [§5] we honor the well-built city of Cyrene, which is held by foreigners who delight in bronze, the Trojan descendants of Antenor. For they came with Helen, after they had seen their native city consumed in the smoke of war.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While the Trojans are described as 'holding' the city, they are explicitly categorized as foreigners (xenoi). In the context of the Greek polis, this often implies a different legal status regarding the soil compared to indigenous or founding lineages.

verbatim: [§9] She did not care for pacing back and forth at the loom, nor for the delights of luncheons with her stay-at-home companions; instead, fighting with bronze javelins and with a sword, she killed wild beasts, providing great restful peace for her father’s cattle.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes Cyrene's rejection of the standard gendered expectations for women in her society (work at the loom and staying at home), establishing these as the normative behaviors for her group.

verbatim: [§10] We shall further praise his noble brothers, because they exalt and strengthen the traditional laws of the Thessaliaaans; the good piloting of states, handed from father to son, rests in the hands of noble men.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Pindar explicitly states that the governance (piloting) of the state is a resource held specifically by the 'noble men' and passed down through lineage.

verbatim: [§10] a man is considered fortunate, and wise poets sing his praises, if he wins victory with his hands or the excellence of his feet, and takes the greatest prizes through his courage and strength, and lives to see his young son duly winning Pythian garlands.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines the specific material and immaterial rewards (prizes, praise, fame) accessible to men of excellence and strength in the context of aristocratic competition.

verbatim: [§11] This is the most hateful error for young brides, and is impossible to conceal because other people will talk. Citizens are apt to speak evil, for prosperity brings with it envy as great as itself.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text suggests that for women (brides), public attention is primarily negative (evil speech/shame), whereas for men it is associated with 'glory'. This indicates a restriction on women's access to positive public renown.

verbatim: [§10] the gorge of Parnassus proclaimed him to the people that live around as the greatest of the boys in the double-course footrace.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a specific resource (participation and victory in the footrace) categorised by the age group 'boys'.

verbatim: and he repaid Polydectes with a deadly wedding-present for the long slavery of his mother and her forced bridal bed
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the mother of Perseus (Danae) being subjected to 'long slavery' and a 'forced bridal bed' by the ruler Polydectes. This indicates that women in this societal context could be legally or de facto coerced into marriage and domestic servitude against their will.

extracted_rules.rule: Protection from enslavement of free women
verbatim: and he repaid Polydectes with a deadly wedding-present for the long slavery of his mother and her forced bridal bed
extracted_rules.reasoning: The reference to 'long slavery' in relation to the mother of a hero indicates that even individuals of lineage could be reduced to a state of slavery or forced labor by those in power, showing a lack of protection against loss of freedom.
